verses.text: Old King David lies cold and feeble, and a young woman, Abishag, is brought to care for him. The mighty warrior is now frail and failing. Even the greatest of God's servants come to weakness and age; the strongest reign ends with a man shivering under blankets.

Adonijah, David's handsome son, exalts himself — 'I will be king' — getting chariots and runners. His father had never once crossed him or asked, 'Why hast thou done so?' A child never disciplined grows into a man who thinks the throne is simply his to take.

Adonijah, David's handsome son, exalts himself — 'I will be king' — getting chariots and runners. His father had never once crossed him or asked, 'Why hast thou done so?' A child never disciplined grows into a man who thinks the throne is simply his to take.

Adonijah wins over Joab and Abiathar and throws a great feast for his allies, but pointedly leaves out Nathan, Benaiah, and Solomon. A plot reveals itself by whom it excludes. Those who scheme for power gather only the agreeable and shut out anyone who would tell them no.

Nathan moves quickly, sending Bathsheba to remind David of his oath that Solomon would reign, while he himself comes to confirm it. The prophet does not panic but acts with wisdom and courage. When God's purpose is threatened, faith responds with timely, careful action.

Bathsheba comes before the aged king and recalls his solemn promise: 'Solomon thy son shall reign after me.' She holds him to his own sworn word. A promise made before God is a debt; the wise plead it humbly and trust the LORD to bring it to pass.

Bathsheba comes before the aged king and recalls his solemn promise: 'Solomon thy son shall reign after me.' She holds him to his own sworn word. A promise made before God is a debt; the wise plead it humbly and trust the LORD to bring it to pass.

Bathsheba comes before the aged king and recalls his solemn promise: 'Solomon thy son shall reign after me.' She holds him to his own sworn word. A promise made before God is a debt; the wise plead it humbly and trust the LORD to bring it to pass.

She warns that Adonijah already reigns, David knowing nothing, and that when the king dies she and Solomon will be counted offenders. The danger is real and near. Those who wait on God's promise may still face a fearful hour before it is fulfilled.

As she speaks, Nathan enters and asks, as if puzzled, whether David has truly named Adonijah king. The prophet's question presses the point home without accusation. Wisely framed truth can move a hesitant heart where blunt demand would only stiffen it.

Nathan reports the feast where they cry 'God save king Adonijah,' and asks whether this was done by the king's authority, behind his servants' backs. He lays the facts before David and leaves the decision to him. Faithful counsel informs, then waits for rightful authority to act.

David rouses himself and swears afresh, 'Solomon thy son shall reign after me'; and Bathsheba bows, 'Let my lord king David live for ever.' The old king's word is still law. Even in his weakness, David honours his oath, and decisive action settles the matter at once.

David rouses himself and swears afresh, 'Solomon thy son shall reign after me'; and Bathsheba bows, 'Let my lord king David live for ever.' The old king's word is still law. Even in his weakness, David honours his oath, and decisive action settles the matter at once.

David rouses himself and swears afresh, 'Solomon thy son shall reign after me'; and Bathsheba bows, 'Let my lord king David live for ever.' The old king's word is still law. Even in his weakness, David honours his oath, and decisive action settles the matter at once.

David orders Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah to set Solomon on the king's own mule, anoint him at Gihon, and sound the trumpet: 'God save king Solomon.' He acts openly and at once. When God's chosen is to be enthroned, half-measures will not do; it is done decisively and in the light.

David orders Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah to set Solomon on the king's own mule, anoint him at Gihon, and sound the trumpet: 'God save king Solomon.' He acts openly and at once. When God's chosen is to be enthroned, half-measures will not do; it is done decisively and in the light.

David orders Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah to set Solomon on the king's own mule, anoint him at Gihon, and sound the trumpet: 'God save king Solomon.' He acts openly and at once. When God's chosen is to be enthroned, half-measures will not do; it is done decisively and in the light.

David charges that Solomon sit on his throne as king in his place, and Benaiah answers with a hearty blessing: may the LORD make Solomon's throne greater even than David's. A good servant rejoices in his master's successor, praying God's blessing on the reign to come.

David charges that Solomon sit on his throne as king in his place, and Benaiah answers with a hearty blessing: may the LORD make Solomon's throne greater even than David's. A good servant rejoices in his master's successor, praying God's blessing on the reign to come.

Solomon is anointed at Gihon, the trumpet sounds, and all the people shout for joy so the earth seems to ring. The rightful king is crowned with gladness. When God's order is followed, the result is not strife but a city rejoicing together with one voice.

Solomon is anointed at Gihon, the trumpet sounds, and all the people shout for joy so the earth seems to ring. The rightful king is crowned with gladness. When God's order is followed, the result is not strife but a city rejoicing together with one voice.

Solomon is anointed at Gihon, the trumpet sounds, and all the people shout for joy so the earth seems to ring. The rightful king is crowned with gladness. When God's order is followed, the result is not strife but a city rejoicing together with one voice.

Adonijah and his guests, just finishing their feast, hear the uproar, and a messenger arrives: David has made Solomon king. The usurper's celebration is shattered by the news. Schemes built against God's purpose collapse the moment the truth breaks in.

The messenger tells it all — Solomon anointed, the city rejoicing, and David blessing God from his bed for letting him see his heir enthroned. The dying king's last joy is to see God's promise kept. To witness the LORD's word fulfilled is comfort enough for the end of life.

Adonijah's guests scatter in terror, and he himself flees to the altar and grabs its horns, begging for his life. The bold usurper of an hour ago is now a frightened man clutching at mercy. Pride collapses fast when the kingdom it grasped at slips away.

Adonijah's guests scatter in terror, and he himself flees to the altar and grabs its horns, begging for his life. The bold usurper of an hour ago is now a frightened man clutching at mercy. Pride collapses fast when the kingdom it grasped at slips away.

Told that Adonijah clings to the altar fearing death, Solomon shows restraint: if he proves worthy, not a hair will fall; if wickedness is found in him, he dies. The new king begins with measured mercy, neither vengeful nor naive. Grace is offered, but on condition of right conduct.
